instructions: Stand with feet shoulder-width apart, arms relaxed at sides. Slowly raise both arms forward to shoulder height with palms facing down. Exhale and gently lower arms back down while bending the knees slightly. Breathe in as arms rise, breathe out as arms lower. Feel the weight sink into the ground. Repeat 2-3 times to settle into the practice.

name_en: Part the Wild Horse's Mane (Left & Right)
name_ru: Расчёсывание гривы дикой лошади (влево и вправо)
instructions: Hold an imaginary ball at chest height with right hand on top. Step forward with left foot into a bow stance while separating hands — left arm sweeps forward and upward to shoulder height, right hand presses down past the hip. Shift weight forward smoothly. Turn torso, gather hands again, step forward with right foot and mirror the movement on the opposite side. Repeat 3 times each side. Coordinate arm movement with the weight shift as one integrated action.

Step back with the left foot while simultaneously raising the right arm upward to face height and lowering the left arm to hip height, palms facing outward in opposite directions. Shift most weight to the back right foot. The front left foot touches lightly with only the toe on the ground. Maintain upright posture with a sense of expansion — as though the crane is opening its wings to achieve balance. Hold the posture for one breath before transitioning.

name_ru: Отвести колено и шагнуть (влево и вправо)
instructions: Swing the right arm out and circle it down past the left knee — brushing it aside — while the left hand simultaneously pushes forward at chest height with a relaxed palm. Step forward with the left foot into a bow stance. Then turn the waist, swing the left arm out and circle down past the right knee while the right hand pushes forward. Step forward with the right foot. Continue alternating sides for 3 repetitions each. The push is relaxed and springy, not forced.

Without taking a full step, draw the back foot a half-step forward and shift most weight to that back foot. The arms take a lute-playing position: one hand rises forward to nose height, the other pulls back near the elbow of the front arm. Both palms face inward toward the body. The posture is still, centered, and meditative. Hold this balanced, quiet form for one full breath before transitioning to the next move.

name_ru: Отшагнуть назад и отбить обезьяну (влево и вправо)
instructions: Step backward with the right foot while the right hand simultaneously draws back past the ear in a circular arc and then pushes forward at shoulder height. The left hand withdraws to the hip as the right pushes forward. Then step backward with the left foot and repeat on the other side, drawing the left hand back and pushing forward. Maintain 4 to 6 backward steps alternating sides. Keep the torso upright and the stepping foot light — place it down toe-first then settle the heel.

Step forward with the left foot. Perform four techniques in flowing sequence: (1) Ward Off — left arm sweeps upward in a curved arc as weight shifts forward, deflecting incoming force; (2) Roll Back — both hands rotate palms-down and pull back across the body as weight shifts to the rear foot, yielding; (3) Press — right palm presses into left wrist and both push forward at chest level as weight shifts forward again; (4) Push Down and Push — hands separate, sink, then both palms thrust forward horizontally. Each technique flows into the next without pause. Breathe naturally.

Mirror of the left-side sequence. Step forward with the right foot and perform Ward Off, Roll Back, Press, and Push on the right side. Ward Off: right arm sweeps upward deflecting outward. Roll Back: both hands rotate and yield, drawing energy back. Press: left palm against right wrist, both push forward. Push: hands separate and both palms thrust forward. Ensure weight shifting is fully coordinated with each hand technique. Breathe in on yielding movements, breathe out on issuing movements.

From a wide stance, shift weight to the right leg and pivot the left foot outward. The right hand forms a crane-beak hook — fingertips pinched together pointing downward — and extends to the right at shoulder height. Step out with the left foot into a bow stance as the left hand arcs upward in front of the face, then opens with palm facing outward and extends to the left at shoulder height. Weight settles on the front left leg. The body is fully open, arms stretched wide like a wide horizon. Hold for one breath.

Stand in a shoulder-width parallel stance or slightly wide horse stance. Hands move in large alternating vertical circles in front of the body — as one hand rises to face height with palm facing inward, the other simultaneously lowers to hip height. Simultaneously side-step with a close-and-open pattern: close feet together as one hand rises, then step out as the other rises. The movement is slow, seamless, and horizontal — drifting sideways like clouds rolling across the sky. Perform 5 to 9 repetitions moving to the left.

Shift nearly all weight to the back foot as the rear hand lifts upward to ear level, then presses or 'pats' gently forward at head height with palm facing down. The front foot lightly touches the ground with only the toes — most weight remains on the back leg. The front hand withdraws to hip level simultaneously. Imagine patting the neck of a tall horse — the movement is light, precise, and graceful rather than heavy or forceful.

Balance on the left foot and cross both hands in front of the chest — right wrist over left. Then separate the hands outward in opposite diagonals while simultaneously lifting the right knee to hip height. Extend the right foot in a slow, controlled heel kick toward the right-forward diagonal. The right hand and right foot extend in the same direction. Maintain an upright torso — do not lean back. Hold the kick position for one breath before slowly lowering the foot back to the ground.

Lower the right foot forward into a left bow stance. Both hands open and drop to the sides at hip level. Then both fists arc upward and inward simultaneously in a hammer-fist motion aimed at the temples of an imaginary opponent — the knuckles face forward at the end. The motion originates from waist rotation, not just arm movement. The strike is focused and controlled, neither limp nor excessively tense. The fists stop at head-level with wrists lightly turned inward.

Turn the body to the left and balance on the right foot. Cross both hands in front of the chest — left wrist over right. Separate hands outward in opposite diagonals while lifting the left knee to hip height. Extend the left foot in a slow, controlled heel kick toward the left-forward diagonal. The left hand and left foot extend in the same direction simultaneously. Keep the supporting right leg slightly bent and stable. Lower the foot gradually with control after the kick.

Transition from Single Whip. The right hand retains the crane-beak hook position extended to the right. Bend the right knee deeply and sink low as the left leg extends outward to the left, foot flat on the ground. The left hand traces a path along the inner side of the left leg from the thigh down toward the foot with palm facing outward. Descend as low as hip flexibility comfortably allows — do not force the depth. The spine remains elongated, not rounded. Breathe out as you sink into the posture.

The left hand forms a crane-beak hook and extends to the left. Bend the left knee deeply and sink low as the right leg extends outward to the right, foot flat on the ground. The right hand traces a path along the inner side of the right extended leg with palm facing outward. Move slowly and with complete muscular control. Ensure the spine stays long rather than collapsing downward. Only go as deep as the body allows today — this posture improves gradually with practice.

name_en: Golden Rooster Stands on One Leg (Left & Right)
name_ru: Золотой петух стоит на одной ноге (влево и вправо)
instructions: Rise up from Snake Creeps Down by lifting one knee high while the same-side arm rises with palm facing up as if lifting the knee from underneath. The opposite arm simultaneously drops straight down to the side. Balance on one leg with the raised knee at or near hip height. The head lifts, the spine is tall. Hold for two to three breaths. Then lower that foot and raise the opposite knee with the opposite arm rising — alternating sides. This posture cultivates deep single-leg rooting and internal strength.

name_ru: Нефритовая дева работает за ткацким станком (четыре угла)
instructions: Perform four diagonal stepping movements, one for each diagonal corner (NE, NW, SW, SE). For each corner: pivot the body, step diagonally with the leading foot into a bow stance, one arm sweeps upward in a ward-off arc to overhead height (palm facing upward as a shield), while the other hand simultaneously pushes forward at chest height with an open palm. After each corner, turn the waist to face the next diagonal before stepping. The four corners represent the four guardians of space. Move with smooth rotational pivots between corners.

Draw the right foot inward so only the toes touch the floor and shift nearly all weight to the rear left foot. One hand — typically the right — drops straight downward with fingers pointing toward the ground, as if threading a very long needle into the sea floor far below. The other left hand draws back near the left hip. The torso tilts slightly forward from the hip joint — the back remains long, not rounded. The body compresses downward like a spring gathering energy for the next movement.

Immediately following Needle at Sea Bottom, step forward with the right foot into a wide bow stance. Simultaneously one arm sweeps upward and diagonally forward — like a fan opening dramatically to the upper-forward diagonal — while the other hand pushes outward at shoulder height in the opposite direction. The body rises and expands fully after the compressed needle posture, releasing accumulated energy in an expansive spreading gesture. The transition from compression to expansion should feel effortless.

Pivot sharply on the heel to turn the body 180 degrees while one fist swings in a downward-deflecting arc clearing the way. Step forward with the left foot, raising the left forearm in a parrying block as the weight shifts forward. Finally, as the body settles into a stable bow stance, the right fist punches straight forward at chest height. Each of the four phases — Turn, Deflect, Parry, Punch — flows directly into the next without any pause. Waist rotation drives each technique. The punch derives power from the step, not just the arm.

Slide both hands under the punching arm with palms facing upward, then separate them forward and outward. Sit back onto the rear foot, drawing both hands back to chest level — palms now face forward in a yielding position as if absorbing incoming force. Then shift weight forward decisively and push both palms outward simultaneously at chest height in a double push. This two-phase withdraw-then-issue movement embodies the core tai chi principle: yield first to gather, then issue to express. Breathe in on the withdrawal, breathe out on the push.

Pivot the body to face the original starting direction. Shift weight to center and step the feet to shoulder-width in a parallel stance with knees slightly bent. Both arms swing open outward to the sides, then arc downward and cross at the wrists in front of the chest — right wrist over left — with palms facing the body. Weight is evenly distributed between both feet. Stand upright and still. Breathe naturally. This posture represents gathering and centering all the energy dispersed during the form, drawing it back to the heart center.

Uncross the hands and lower both arms slowly and smoothly down to the sides with palms facing downward. Gradually straighten the knees as the arms lower — do not lock the knees. Return to the neutral shoulder-width standing position that mirrored the Opening Form. Breathe out fully and completely as the arms lower all the way. Stand quietly for one full minute after the arms rest — do not rush away. Absorb the calm energy cultivated throughout the entire form before resuming normal activity.
